transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Good evening everyone. Sprint 4 retrospective. I said in this morning's standup I'd schedule this today and here we are. UAT starts Monday. Sprint is effectively closed. Let's spend an hour doing this properly. I sent the agenda around noon — what went well, what didn't, and real action items for Sprint 5. Let's start with what went well. Rakesh?

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I'll start with the one that matters most to me — team resilience. Day 1 of this sprint the environment was blocked. Day 3 the demo failed in front of the client and BU head got cc'd on communication. Day 4 we found a prod SSL cert mismatch that could have killed the go-live. Under that kind of pressure, teams either fragment or they pull together. This team pulled together every single time. That's not something you take for granted.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: The async queue. I want to call that out specifically. It wasn't in the original Sprint 4 plan. Mid-sprint we identified it as a critical gap — not just for Sprint 4, but as a dependency the entire Sprint 5 returns flow rests on. We identified the risk, documented it formally, got client buy-in, got infra provisioned, built it properly with real Redis integration, and it resolved the 2 intermittent prod failures that had been open since Day 5. That was good technical problem-solving under real pressure.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: 34 of 34 passing at close of sprint. That number matters because of where we started — Day 1 we had zero tests running because the environment wasn't up. By the end, full test coverage, all passing, including edge cases I discovered during UAT preparation that weren't in the original scenario list. The double-payment scenario, the browser-close flow, the slow-connection spinner — none of those were in my original 34. They got found and fixed before client saw them.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: The cross-functional session yesterday — Varsha, Ajay and I, going through edge cases together for about two hours. That single session found four issues that would have been UAT failures on Monday. We found the double-payment gap, the browser back button problem, the slow connection spinner, and the transaction reconciliation case when user closes the browser. Two hours of collaborative work. We should not need a crisis to make that happen. It should be a sprint ritual.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Redis stability once it was provisioned. Zero issues from Wednesday 2pm to now — 40-plus hours. The monitoring alert I set up has not fired once. Infrastructure, when it's done properly, becomes invisible. The fact that it hasn't been mentioned since Wednesday is the point.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good. Now what didn't go well. I want the unvarnished version.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Day 1. Staging was not functional. Monday morning, start of a sprint with a Wednesday go-live target. The infra ticket was raised the Friday before the sprint started. I flagged it explicitly in the Sprint 4 kickoff call. Central infra didn't apply the config until 1:20pm Monday. We lost an entire morning on Day 1. And this is not new — the Sprint 3 retro had the exact same issue. We said we'd fix it. We didn't fully fix it.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: 4 hours lost on Day 1 of a 10-day sprint with a fixed Wednesday go-live. That's not a minor inconvenience. That's a significant portion of the testing window. I had 34 test cases ready from Day 1 and couldn't run any of them until 2pm.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: The Wednesday demo failure. I want to be honest about this one. Varsha had deployed the timeout fix and retry logic to staging-v2. Ajay ran the happy path on staging-v2 and it passed. But the demo was on staging-v1, which hadn't received the deployment. Client saw the error screen. BU head got added to all communication. That's the most damaging single event of the sprint.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: That's on me. I assumed the demo was on staging-v2 because that's where we'd been working. I didn't verify. A 2-minute check before the demo would have caught it. We now have a single deployment pipeline that keeps both environments in sync — Vivek built that Wednesday noon. But the culture of verify-before-demo needs to be a checklist item, not an assumption, going forward.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: The SSL cert issue on Day 4. I want to name this directly. I raised the prod-staging SSL cert mismatch in an infra sync meeting two weeks before this sprint started. It was noted. Nobody followed up on it. Not infra, not me, not anyone on this team. Thursday morning I checked prod independently, found the cert authority was completely different, and we nearly went into a production payment go-live with 28 tests passing on staging that would have all failed on prod. We were one day away from that.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: I was in that infra sync meeting where Vivek raised the SSL concern. I heard it and I moved on. That's on me too. It wasn't just an infra failure — it was a team failure to treat a flagged risk as a real item that needed ownership and follow-through.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: The intermittent prod failures. I want to trace the full history of this. Day 2 — I was testing from 2pm, saw 1 in 5 requests timing out, messaged the group at midnight. Varsha identified the 3-second timeout as the root cause. Day 3 we fixed timeout and added retry logic. Day 5 on prod, I'm still seeing 2 of 34 tests intermittently failing. They persisted all of Week 2 until the async queue was deployed Thursday night. Those 2 cases were in prod with real transactions for 7 days before they were resolved. We knew about them. We had a risk documented. But prod was running with a known failure mode for a week.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: That connects directly to the decision to descope the async retry queue in Sprint 4. At the time descoping it felt reasonable — the gateway-down scenario only happens in edge cases. But what I said in Tuesday's standup was accurate: we had two more intermittent failures in prod Tuesday evening, between 7 and 9pm, peak usage time. We were lucky that volume was low. Three real transactions over the weekend, none hit the failure mode. With higher volume in UAT next week, that luck would have run out.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Scope management mid-sprint. Sprint 4 started with a clear scope — payment API end to end. By the end we had also added: async retry queue, idempotency check, browser close handling, reconciliation logic on page reload, and the UAT preparation materials. Each individual addition made sense. But they weren't going through any formal scope change process — they were informal absorb-and-adjust. The team just took them on without anyone calculating the total impact.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: And some of those additions came in through side channels — a comment in a standup, a message in the group chat. I wasn't always sure whether something was in scope for this sprint or if it was going to next sprint's backlog. That ambiguity cost me at least two context switches that disrupted a full half-day each.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good. These are real problems with real root causes. Now — action items for Sprint 5. I want commitments, not aspirations.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: My commitment — mandatory environment parity checklist before sprint Day 1. Before the first standup, I run a comparison between staging and prod: SSL cert authority, network config, DB config, environment variables. If anything doesn't match, I flag it immediately. The checklist is written down, not in my head. And I'll co-own it with Ajay — two people sign off, not one.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I'll co-own the environment checklist with Vivek — agreed. And pre-demo verification becomes a mandatory 5-minute ritual before any client-facing session. Environment name, deployment version, one smoke test result. Written, not assumed. If the check isn't done, the demo doesn't start.

transcript.speaker: Varsha
transcript.text: SSL cert rotation calendar. Vivek and I will set up a shared calendar with all certificate expiry dates and a 4-week advance renewal reminder. Both of us have visibility. No more discovering cert issues when something breaks. Vivek, I'll set this up with you this weekend — it's maybe 30 minutes.

transcript.speaker: Vivek
transcript.text: Yes. Let's do it Saturday. And I'll also finally build the config drift detection script I've been promising since Sprint 3 retro. Daily automated check comparing staging and prod key values. Runs at 8:30am, posts results to the team channel. If there's drift, I know before standup, not after Ajay's first test fails.

transcript.speaker: Rupali
transcript.text: Scope freeze and formal change process. From Sprint 5 — scope is locked 3 days before sprint start. Any mid-sprint addition goes to Rakesh first for effort estimate, then to me for scope decision. If it's added to this sprint, something of equivalent size gets deferred. If it's under 2 hours it can be absorbed silently. Above 2 hours it needs a formal decision. No more informal absorb-and-adjust.

transcript.speaker: Rakesh
transcript.text: I want to add the risk register. Any technical risk flagged by anyone — in a standup, in a sync meeting, in a Slack message — goes into a shared risk register within 24 hours. Owner assigned, review date assigned. Vivek's SSL flag two weeks ago should have been in a risk register with a resolution target date. If it had been, someone on this team would have followed up before Day 4 of the sprint. I'll create the template tonight and share it Monday morning.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: Good. I'll reinforce the scope freeze and risk register expectations directly with the client. They need to hear it from me. They can't drop three large scope items on us via Friday evening email and expect the next sprint to absorb them. That's not how we'll work in Sprint 5.

transcript.speaker: Suraj
transcript.text: I want to formally propose the cross-functional edge case session as a sprint ceremony. Sprint day 5 or 6, two hours, backend plus frontend plus QA together, go through edge cases that each side might not have thought of. The session Varsha, Ajay and I did yesterday — two hours found four UAT-level issues. That has to be repeatable.

transcript.speaker: Ajay
transcript.text: I'll add it to the sprint calendar. Day 6, 2 hours. Mandatory. Not optional when people are busy. The busier we are, the more we need it.

transcript.speaker: Vinod
transcript.text: I'll close this. Sprint 4 was the hardest sprint on this project. We had more things go wrong than any previous sprint — environment failure Day 1, demo failure Day 3, SSL cert Day 4, partial go-live Day 5, scope escalation from client Day 8. But the output is strong: 34 of 34 tests passing, async queue built and in prod, client relationship intact, UAT ready for Monday. That's what this team produced under those conditions. I'm telling you that matters. Let's go into Sprint 5 with these specific lessons and not repeat them. Good work this sprint.
